name: Jason Hart
education: Juris Doctor, University of Kansas School of Law
whoTheyAre:
Jason Hart is a former federal prosecutor from Wichita who spent more than 21 years working in criminal justice. After law school at the University of Kansas, he started his legal career in Dodge City before moving to the Shawnee County District Attorney's Office. He then served in the Kansas Attorney General's Office and spent 16 years as an Assistant United States Attorney for the District of Kansas. During that time, he focused on child exploitation and cybercrime cases, working under both Democratic and Republican presidential administrations.

Hart says the decision to run for office came after "prayerful and tearful discussions" with his wife and family. The January 2026 killing of Alex Pretti by federal agents in Minneapolis was the event that pushed him to leave his government career and enter politics for the first time. He resigned from his federal job in early March 2026 and filed for the U.S. Senate race shortly after.

Hart had no prior political experience before this campaign. He originally planned to challenge U.S. Rep. Ron Estes in the Republican primary for the 4th Congressional District, but switched course after learning a friend was already running in that race as a Democrat. He changed his party registration to Democrat and filed for the Senate instead.
theirRecord:
Hart does not have a voting record from elected office, so his public record is based entirely on his career as a prosecutor and his early campaign statements. His strongest credential is his 16-year tenure as a federal prosecutor protecting children from exploitation and fighting cybercrime. He served under multiple administrations from both parties, which he points to as evidence that he puts public service above partisanship.

On the other hand, Hart entered the race very late in March 2026, with no campaign manager and no fundraising infrastructure in place. His party registration changed twice in a single month -- from independent to Republican in February 2026, then from Republican to Democrat in March 2026. While he frames this as following his conscience, opponents could use the rapid switches to question his political convictions. He has also described himself as "white, male, Christian, hetero, 2A-friendly, LEO-friendly, and very clearly anti-pedo" as reasons he is the strongest Democratic candidate, which is an unusual pitch that could attract some crossover voters but alienate parts of the Democratic base.
whatYouShouldKnow: Hart spent 16 years as an Assistant U.S. Attorney prosecuting child exploitation and cybercrime cases under both Democratic and Republican administrations.
He has never held elected office. This is his first political campaign.
He switched his party registration twice in one month -- from independent to Republican in February 2026, then to Democrat in March 2026.
He quit his federal prosecutor job to run, entering the race in early March 2026 with no campaign manager and no established fundraising operation.
He says the killing of Alex Pretti by federal agents in January 2026 was the catalyst for his decision to run, following prayerful conversations with his wife and family.

He positions himself as a Democrat who can win over disillusioned Republicans, calling them voters "lost in the wilderness" who are looking for someone they can vote for rather than just vote against.
He describes himself as pro-Second Amendment, pro-law enforcement, and Christian -- an unusual profile for a Democratic Senate candidate in Kansas.
No campaign finance totals have been publicly reported yet. His campaign is still in its earliest stages of fundraising.
whereTheyWorship: Hart publicly identifies as Christian and has used faith-based language to describe his decision to run, saying the choice came after "prayerful and tearful discussions" with his wife and family. However, no specific church, denomination, or congregation has been identified in any public reporting or campaign materials. His faith appears to be a genuine part of his personal life, but he has not publicly named where he worships.

quotes.text: There are many Republicans who are in the wilderness right now. They are sort of like, 'the Republican Party that I grew up with is not the Republican Party right now,' and they are really struggling with where their place is in the world. And they're looking for someone who is someone they can vote for and not simply vote against.

I ask people questions for a living, and that's what we need more of, not talking at people. I listen to the experts, and the experts are our Kansas voters, our Kansas constituents, our Kansas communities that can tell us exactly what they need, and then take that and go to D.C. and advocate for them.
The Alex Pretti killing led to prayerful and tearful discussions with my wife and family before deciding to run.

campaignFinance.narrative: Hart entered the Senate race in early March 2026, making him the latest major entrant in the Democratic primary. As of late March 2026, no specific fundraising totals have appeared in public reporting or FEC filings. He did not have a campaign manager at the time of his most prominent media profile. His campaign website and an ActBlue donation page are the main public fundraising channels identified so far, suggesting a campaign still building its financial infrastructure from the ground up.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: No donor information is publicly available at this stage of his campaign.
